full_response:
Let  

    X_t  := length of the line at the beginning of minute t,       t = 0,1,2,…

The chain starts in state 0 and evolves as follows.

• If X_t = 0  
     – with probability p, two friends arrive, Barr serves one of them, so X_{t+1}=1;  
     – with probability 1-p, nobody arrives and nobody is served, so X_{t+1}=0.

• If X_t ≥ 1  
     – with probability p, two friends arrive, the front friend is served, so X_{t+1}=X_t+1;  
     – with probability 1-p, nobody arrives, one person is served, so X_{t+1}=X_t-1.

Thus for every i ≥ 1

     P(i → i+1)=p ,   P(i → i-1)=1-p,

and for i = 0

     P(0 → 1)=p ,   P(0 → 0)=1-p.

This is the simple, one–dimensional, reflecting random walk on the non–negative
integers with “right” step-probability p.

----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Deriving the stationary equations
----------------------------------------------------------------
Because the chain is a birth–death chain with only nearest–neighbour moves,
the detailed-balance (reversibility) equations are necessary and sufficient
for stationarity:

     π_i P(i → i+1) = π_{i+1} P(i+1 → i) ,      i ≥ 0.                  (1)

Substituting the transition probabilities gives

     π_i · p = π_{i+1} · (1-p)                                          (2)

⇒   π_{i+1} = (p/(1-p)) · π_i .                                        (3)

Iterating (3) yields, for every i ≥ 0,

     π_i = π_0 · (p/(1-p))^{\,i}.                                      (4)

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  When does a normalisable solution exist?
----------------------------------------------------------------
To be a probability distribution we must have Σ_{i=0}^{∞} π_i = 1:

     π_0 Σ_{i=0}^{∞} (p/(1-p))^{\,i} = 1                               (5)

The infinite geometric series in (5) converges iff the ratio r := p/(1-p)
is < 1, i.e. iff

     p < 1/2.                                                          (6)

When p ≥ 1/2 the series diverges, hence no finite-mass distribution solves
the stationary equations; the chain is then null-recurrent (p = 1/2) or
transient (p > 1/2).

----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The stationary distribution for 0 < p < 1/2
----------------------------------------------------------------
Under condition (6) the sum in (5) equals 1/(1-r).  Therefore

     π_0 = 1-r = 1 - p/(1-p) = (1-2p)/(1-p).                          (7)

Inserting π_0 into (4) gives, for every i ≥ 0,

     π_i = (1-2p)/(1-p) · (p/(1-p))^{\,i}.                            (8)

Because 0 < p < 1/2, every π_i in (8) is non–negative and Σ_i π_i = 1,
so (8) is indeed a probability distribution and is the unique stationary
distribution of the chain.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion
----------------------------------------------------------------
The line-length Markov chain possesses a stationary distribution iff
p < 1/2.  In that case the stationary probabilities decay geometrically
as in (8).

\boxed{\text{A stationary distribution exists iff }0<p<\tfrac12,\;
\text{and then } 
\displaystyle \pi_i=\frac{1-2p}{1-p}\Bigl(\frac{p}{1-p}\Bigr)^{i},
\; i=0,1,2,\dots}

Confidence: 75%
